title: Overeating and that stuffed, indigested feeling
excerpt: Most of us have felt that heavy, bloated, can't-move feeling after eating too much. Ayurveda has a precise name for it, a clear explanation of what went wrong, and a simple plan for the day after.
at_a_glance: Ajirna means indigestion from a fire overwhelmed by too much food, not just discomfort.
The classical rule: fill half with food, one quarter with liquid, leave one quarter empty.
The day after heavy eating, warm water and genuine hunger are your two best tools.
agni_desc: The goal is a steady, balanced fire (sama). Too low leaves food undigested; too sharp burns through it. An irregular fire (vishama) swings between the two.

body.heading: that stuffed feeling has a name
paragraphs: You know the feeling. The wedding thali was too good, or the family dinner went one helping too far, and now you are sitting very still, willing your stomach to cooperate. Ayurveda calls this state Ajirna, which translates simply as indigestion or the failure of proper digestion. It is not just discomfort. The classical texts treat it as one of the root causes of a long list of diseases, which is why they spend considerable effort explaining exactly how it happens and what to do about it.

The central idea in Ayurveda is that your digestive fire, called Agni, has a certain capacity. When you eat within that capacity, food is transformed into nourishment. When you exceed it, the fire is overwhelmed and food is left in an intermediate, half-processed state. Caraka Samhita, one of the oldest and most authoritative Ayurvedic texts, describes this unprocessed material as Ama, a kind of sticky, toxic residue that underlies almost all disease (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). Ajirna is essentially the moment when Ama begins to form.
body.pullQuote: That last quarter is not wasted space. It is what allows the digestive system to churn and move food efficiently.

body.heading: the right quantity: what the granthas say
paragraphs: The classical texts are surprisingly practical about quantity. Caraka Samhita discusses the concept of Matra, or the appropriate measure of food, and states that the right quantity is the amount that digests comfortably within time, without disturbing the body's natural rhythms or causing heaviness (Caraka Samhita, Vimana Sthana). Ashtanga Hridaya, a more concise classical text widely used in Kerala's tradition, echoes this and adds that one should fill the stomach roughly half with food, one quarter with liquid, and leave one quarter empty for the movement of Vata, the air-like force that drives digestion (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). That last quarter is not wasted space. It is what allows the digestive system to churn and move food efficiently.

Madhava Nidana, the classical text focused on diagnosis, describes several types of Ajirna based on which dosha is most disturbed. Ama-Ajirna, where food simply sits undigested and heavy, is most common after overeating. Vidagdha-Ajirna, with burning and sourness, tends to follow spicy or rich food taken in excess. Vistabdha-Ajirna, marked by gas, bloating, and a sense of stagnation, results from large quantities of dry or Vata-aggravating foods (Madhava Nidana, Ajirna Nidana). Recognising which type you are experiencing helps you choose the right remedy rather than guessing.

body.heading: resetting after a heavy meal: gentle, practical habits
paragraphs: The Ayurvedic response to Ajirna is not a harsh cleanse or a complicated protocol. It is, above all, restraint and rest for the digestive fire. The single most important instruction in the classical texts is Langhana, which means lightening or fasting. This does not mean starving yourself. It means giving Agni a quiet day to catch up. On the day after heavy eating, classical guidance suggests warm water or light soups, skipping one meal if hunger is genuinely absent, and avoiding anything cold, raw, or heavy (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana).

Warm water is mentioned repeatedly in Ashtanga Hridaya as a direct aid to digestion: it kindles Agni, helps move Ama, and reduces the bloated, stagnant feeling that follows overeating (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Sipping warm water through the morning, rather than cold water or juice, is one of the simplest and most evidence-backed habits you can adopt. A small amount of fresh ginger, either grated into warm water or chewed before a light meal, is a classic Ayurvedic remedy to rekindle digestive fire without adding to the load on the stomach. Cumin and coriander steeped in hot water and strained into a drink, known informally as a digestive tea in many Indian households, works along the same lines.

Physical rest helps, but a short, slow walk of ten to fifteen minutes after a meal is actually encouraged in classical texts as an aid to digestion. What the texts caution strongly against is sleeping immediately after eating, exercising strenuously right after a heavy meal, or suppressing natural urges like belching, which are the body's own pressure-release mechanisms (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). These are not old wives' tales. They reflect a coherent understanding of how digestive physiology responds to load.

The bigger habit the texts advocate is paying attention to hunger before you eat, not just appetite. Hunger, in the Ayurvedic sense, means the previous meal has been digested and Agni is ready to receive food again. Eating before that point, even if the food is light, compounds the problem. The practical rule: wait until you feel genuinely light and hungry, with a clear mouth and easy breathing, before sitting down to the next meal after a heavy one.

body.heading: when to see a doctor or vaidya
paragraphs: The tips above are suited to the ordinary, occasional overeating that most of us experience. Please consult a doctor or qualified Ayurvedic vaidya if your indigestion is severe, lasts more than two or three days, or keeps coming back without an obvious cause. Seek medical attention promptly if you notice blood in your stool or vomit, persistent vomiting, sharp or radiating abdominal pain, unexplained weight loss, difficulty swallowing, or a feeling of fullness after eating very little. These can be signs of conditions that need proper diagnosis and treatment, not home remedies. This article is general information for healthy adults and is not a substitute for medical advice, diagnosis, or care.

try_this_today.title: Sip warm water through the morning
try_this_today.description: After a heavy meal or on the morning after, avoid cold water and juice. Sip plain warm water through the first few hours. It gently kindles Agni without adding digestive load.

try_this_today.title: Take a short, slow walk after eating
try_this_today.description: Ten to fifteen minutes of easy walking after a meal supports digestion. Avoid strenuous exercise and resist the urge to lie down for at least an hour after eating.

try_this_today.title: Try a simple digestive tea
try_this_today.description: Add half a teaspoon each of cumin and coriander seeds to two cups of water. Bring to a boil, simmer for five minutes, then strain and sip warm. This is a classic household remedy for the heavy, bloated feeling.

try_this_today.title: Wait for true hunger before the next meal
try_this_today.description: After overeating, skip the next meal if you are not genuinely hungry. Ayurveda's cue for real hunger: you feel light, your mouth is clear, and your breathing is easy. Eating before that point adds to the burden.

try_this_today.title: Chew a small piece of fresh ginger before a light meal
try_this_today.description: A thin slice of fresh ginger with a pinch of salt, chewed slowly about ten minutes before eating, is a traditional way to wake up a sluggish Agni before a light recovery meal.

ingredients.name: Ginger
ingredients.description: Kindles Agni and relieves nausea and heaviness after overeating.

ingredients.name: Cumin
ingredients.description: A classic digestive spice that reduces bloating and supports the breakdown of food.

ingredients.name: Coriander
ingredients.description: Cooling and carminative; paired with cumin in digestive teas to ease Pitta-type indigestion.

words_to_know.definition: Indigestion; the state in which food is not properly digested because the digestive fire has been overwhelmed.
The digestive fire; the force responsible for transforming food into nourishment and waste.
Unprocessed, sticky residue left behind when Agni cannot complete digestion; seen in Ayurveda as a root cause of disease.
Lightening or therapeutic reduction; in the context of digestion, it means eating less or fasting lightly to give Agni space to recover.
The appropriate, individualised measure of food; the quantity that can be digested comfortably without strain.
The air-and-space force in Ayurveda that governs movement, including the movement of food through the digestive tract.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana · Caraka Samhita, Vimana Sthana · Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana · Madhava Nidana, Ajirna Nidana
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
